title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The PR introduces two useful endpoints but ships with several must-fix issues: a PCI-risk field that could surface full card numbers, a module-level refunds store that pollutes tests and is shared across service instances, a completely broken sort (wrong property name, silently compares undefined values), a 200 OK returned when a transaction is not found, and a silent dollar→cent heuristic that masks bad input instead of rejecting it. Several should-fix issues follow: no over-refund guard, an unbounded full-table scan with an N+1 loop inside it, and test assertions so weak they would pass even if every endpoint returned 500. The code would benefit from tighter validation, pagination hooks, and use of the existing structured logger instead of bare console.log.

findings.title: card_number field may leak full PAN in API response
findings.message: The response object includes a `card_number` key populated by `(tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4`. The double cast to `unknown` bypasses TypeScript's type safety specifically to reach a raw `card_number` property. If a full card number ever lands on the transaction object (e.g. from a gateway response, a future refactor, or a debugging session), it will be silently returned to every caller of this endpoint. The field should be removed entirely; the already-present `card_last4` key on the same object is sufficient and safe.

findings.title: Customer ID logged via console.log — PII leak
findings.message: `console.log('history requested for customer ' + customerId)` writes a customer identifier to stdout in plain text. The same pattern repeats in `refundsService.ts` line 24 with a transaction ID. The project already depends on `pino-http`; use the request-scoped logger (`req.log.info(...)`) so that log levels, redaction, and structured output are enforced consistently. Remove or replace all bare `console.log` calls.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore is a shared singleton — pollutes tests
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` lives at module scope and is never reset. Every `createApp()` call inside the test suite shares the same store, so refunds created in one test bleed into another. In production this also means all `RefundsService` instances share one store, which is the opposite of the per-app isolation that `InMemoryTransactionRepository` (an instance field) provides. Move `refundsStore` to an instance field on `RefundsService` so each app instance (and each test) gets its own store.

findings.title: Sort compares tx.created_at which is undefined — order is always unchanged
findings.message: The `Transaction` domain type uses `createdAt` (camelCase), not `created_at`. Both comparisons evaluate `undefined < undefined` and `undefined > undefined`, which both return `false`, so the comparator always returns 0 and the array is never reordered. The comment even acknowledges the mismatch ("matches our response field name") but that doesn't fix the domain field name. Fix: sort on `a.createdAt` and `b.createdAt` and remove the `any` casts.

findings.title: Silent dollar→cent conversion masks invalid amounts instead of rejecting them
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount`, the code tries to "auto-correct" by multiplying by 100 and re-checking. This means a client that sends `{ amount: 15 }` when the charge was `1500` cents gets a full refund silently, rather than an error. The caller has no indication the amount was changed. Financial APIs must be strict: if the requested amount exceeds the transaction amount, return a 422 with a clear message. Remove this heuristic entirely.

findings.title: Returns 200 OK with empty body when transaction is not found
findings.message: When `result == null` (transaction ID not found), the controller returns `res.status(200).json({})`. Clients cannot distinguish "refund succeeded" from "transaction not found". The correct status is `404 Not Found` with a descriptive error body matching the project's existing error format. Also replace the loose `==` with `=== null || result === undefined` or simply `!result`.

findings.title: No over-refund guard — multiple refunds can exceed the original charge
findings.message: After the per-refund amount check, there is no check against the sum of all previous refunds for the same transaction. `listRefundsForTransaction` is already available; compute `previouslyRefunded = existingRefunds.reduce((s, r) => s + r.amount, 0)` and reject (422) if `previouslyRefunded + refundAmount > tx.amount`. Without this, a transaction can be refunded multiple times for its full amount.

findings.title: findAll() loads every transaction into memory — no pagination
findings.message: `this.transactions.findAll()` returns the entire transaction set, then filters in application code. As the data grows this will consume unbounded memory and CPU. The `TransactionRepository` interface should gain a `findByCustomerId(id, options: { limit, cursor })` method so filtering and pagination happen at the storage layer. At minimum add a hard cap (e.g. 1 000 records) and return a `next_cursor` to callers.

findings.title: N+1 refund lookups — O(n × m) scan per request
findings.message: For each of the customer's transactions, `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` is awaited sequentially, and each call does a full linear scan of the entire `refundsStore`. With T transactions and R total refunds the cost is O(T × R). Fix by loading all relevant refunds in a single pass (e.g. `listRefundsForTransaction` batch variant) and grouping them by `transactionId` in a Map before the loop.

findings.title: Test assertions are too weak — failures are invisible
findings.message: Three assertions across the test suite are effectively no-ops: (1) `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` — any response including a 500 error has a body. Assert `expect(refund.status).toBe(201)` and spot-check `refund.body.refund_id` and `refund.body.amount`. (2) `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` — should be `toBe(404)` once the missing-transaction bug above is fixed. (3) The history test never creates any transactions for `cus_abc` first, so it only tests the empty-list path. Add charge → history → assert structure tests.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema is dead code — never imported
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is defined here but not imported or used anywhere in the diff. The route uses `req.params.id` directly without validation. Either delete the schema or wire it up via a middleware to validate that `:id` is non-empty.

findings.title: Loose equality == used for string comparison
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` should be `tx.customerId === customerId`. Both sides are typed as `string`; the loose equality adds no value and is inconsistent with strict TypeScript/ESLint conventions typical in Express projects.
